coredata.dc:title: Preparation of N-doped carbon dots and application to enhanced photosynthesis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Jinzhi
Yang, Wenli
Miao, Yanming
coredata.dc:description: Regulation of photosynthesis rates is one of the key ways to increase crop yields. Carbon dots (CDs), which are low-toxity and biocompatible optical nanomaterials, can be easily prepared and are ideal for improving photosynthesis efficiency. In this study, nitrogen-doped CDs (CNDs) with a fluorescent quantum yield of 0.36 were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. These CNDs can convert a part of ultraviolet light in solar energy to blue light (emission peak at 410 nm) that can be utilized in photosynthesis and that overlaps with the optical absorption spectrum of chloroplasts in the blue light zone. Consequently, chloroplasts can pick up photons excited by the CNDs and transfer them to the photosynthetic system in the form of electrons, thereby accelerating the photoelectron transport rate. These behaviors can reduce ultraviolet light stress on wheat seedlings and improve the efficiency of electron capture and transfer from chloroplasts through optical energy conversion. As a result, various photosynthetic indices and biomass of wheat seedlings are improved. Cytotoxicity experiments have showed that CNDs within a certain concentration range almost do not affect cell survival.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Photosynthesis
Wheat seedlings

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence properties of CNDs promote photosynthesis of wheat seedlings

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was completed under the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant 32001202 , 32171393 and 31700862 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of CNDs.

Characterization of CNDs: (A) TEM image (inset: particle size distribution); (B) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) image; (C) C 1 s spectrum, (D) O 1 s spectrum, (E) N 1 s high-resolution spectrum; (F) 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum.

Fluorescent properties of CNDs in aqueous solutions: (A) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs (inset: environmental light (left) and CNDs photographed under 365 nm ultraviolet ray (right)); (B) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence excitation spectrum of CNDs; (C) tangents at abrupt change points and absorption edge positions on UV–vis spectra of CNDs; (D) 3D fluorescence spectrum of CNDs; (E) fluorescent lifetime of CNDs.

Effects of CNDs on the photosynthesis of wheat seedlings; (A) experimental schematic diagram about effects of CNDs on photosynthesis of wheat seedlings (CK, control blank); (B) absorption spectrum (green) of chloroplast and emission spectrum (red) of CNDs; (C) effects of CNDs on photosynthetic rate per unit area and photosynthetic nitrogen use efficiency by wheat seedlings; (D) effects of CNDs on root length and leaf area of wheat seedlings; (E) effects of times of CNDs (20 mg/ml) brushing wheat seedling leaves on dry weight of seedling roots, dry weight of leaves, and dry biomass; (F) effects of times of CNDs (20 mg/ml) brushing wheat seedling leaves on fresh weight of seedling roots, fresh weight of leaves, and fresh biomass; (G) effects of times of CNDs (20 mg/ml) brushing wheat seedling leaves on specific leaf areas of wheat seedlings; (H) effects of concentration of CNDs (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 mg/ml) and illumination time on DCPIP use by wheat seedlings; (I) effects of chloroplast and chloroplast/CNDs compound on iron cyanide reduced quantity and ATP concentration; (J) effects of chloroplast and chloroplast/CNDs compound on Rubisco activity; (K) CNDs (linked with Cy3.5) the fluorescence microscope image of wheat seedling leaves smeared and washed and the scanning electron microscope image of wheat seedling leaves smeared and washed by N-CDs, (a) Blank, (b) 10 times magnification, (c) 20 times magnification, (d, e, f) are scanning electron micrographs; (L) schematic diagram on CNDs enhancing photosynthesis of wheat seedlings. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effects of CNDs on activity of Hela cells in vitro: measurement by MTT method. (A) laser confocal microscopic images of Hela cells incubated with (a) Cy3.5 or (b) Cy3.5-marked CNDs for 1, 4, 8, 16, 24, 48 h, cell microfilaments (MF) stained by phalloidin (green), nuclei stained by DAPI (blue), scale at 20 µm; (B) effects of CND concentration (0, 2, 200, 2000, 5000, 10000 μg/mL) on the activity of Hela cells; (C) Effect of PBS and CNDs on cell ROS DCFH-DA signal; (D) effects of PBS and CNDs on cell apoptosis (ratio of apoptotic cells%). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different carbon dots promoting plant photosynthetic characteristics.

row.entry: Increased root, shoot weight, increased net photosynthetic rate
Increased root, shoot weight, increased net photosynthetic rate
Increased root, shoot weight, increased net photosynthetic rate
Increased root, shoot weight, increased net photosynthetic rate

head.ce:title: Preparation of N-doped carbon dots and application to enhanced photosynthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped CDs (CNDs) with a fluorescent quantum yield of 0.36 were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. These CNDs can convert a part of ultraviolet light in solar energy to blue light (emission peak at 410 nm). These behaviors can reduce ultraviolet light stress on wheat seedlings and improve the efficiency of electron capture and transfer from chloroplasts through optical energy conversion. As a result, various photosynthetic indices and biomass of wheat seedlings are improved. Cytotoxicity experiments have showed that CNDs within a certain concentration range almost do not affect cell survival.

Regulation of photosynthesis rates is one of the key ways to increase crop yields. Carbon dots (CDs), which are low-toxity and biocompatible optical nanomaterials, can be easily prepared and are ideal for improving photosynthesis efficiency. In this study, nitrogen-doped CDs (CNDs) with a fluorescent quantum yield of 0.36 were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. These CNDs can convert a part of ultraviolet light in solar energy to blue light (emission peak at 410 nm) that can be utilized in photosynthesis and that overlaps with the optical absorption spectrum of chloroplasts in the blue light zone. Consequently, chloroplasts can pick up photons excited by the CNDs and transfer them to the photosynthetic system in the form of electrons, thereby accelerating the photoelectron transport rate. These behaviors can reduce ultraviolet light stress on wheat seedlings and improve the efficiency of electron capture and transfer from chloroplasts through optical energy conversion. As a result, various photosynthetic indices and biomass of wheat seedlings are improved. Cytotoxicity experiments have showed that CNDs within a certain concentration range almost do not affect cell survival.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Improving optical energy utilization rate is one of the key ways to increase crop yields. During crop photosynthesis, the solar light utilization by chloroplasts is limited to the visible light range with specific absorptivity [1]. In fact, less than 10 % of the sun’s light can be used effectively. The ultraviolet (UV) light, accounting for 8.7 % of total solar energy, is almost not used by farming plants and even threatens or injures crops, decreasing the photosynthesis efficiency and productivity of plants. Thus, it is crucial to construct artificial interventions to increase the solar capture and conversion rate of crops and reduce the threat of UV light. Specifically, development of light conversion materials, which can maximally convert the unusable light by crops to usable visible light by crops, is an effective measure to improve the photosynthesis efficiency of crops [2]. Among them, increasing the proportion of blue light during photosynthesis is beneficial for improving the proportion of plant protein synthesis,which is conducive to improving crop quality.

According to the rationale of photosynthesis, the use of fluorescent materials (e.g., organic fluorescent molecules [3–4], inorganic metal fluorescent quantum dots [5]) to regulate light capture, electron transfer and energy conversion during photosynthesis is considered as one ideal way to improve photosynthesis efficiency. However, the fluorescence emission of organic fluorescent molecules is unstable and quenchable [6–7], which restricts their practical applications. Compared with organic molecules, inorganic metal fluorescent quantum dots have broader spectral regions, more efficient excited energy conversion, and more stable fluorescence emission. Nevertheless, fluorescent quantum dots usually contain heavy metal ions that are highly toxic to organisms [8–9].

Carbon dots (CDs) as novel photoluminescent nanomaterials are featured by simple, preparation, low costs, stable fluorescence emission, low toxicity and high biocompatibility and are extensively used in photoelectric devices [10–11], sensors [12–13], catalysis [14–15], bacterial prevention [16–17] and forgery prevention [18–19]. Especially, the nano-bio effect of CDs is one hot spot of research on applications of CDs, such as the utilization of optical properties of CDs into photosynthesis [20–21]. CDs are outstanding with high biocompatibility. CDs, a potential type of light-capturing nanomaterials, can convert the unusable light into usable visible light by plants and thus improve the light use efficiency of plants [20–24]. However, most photosynthetic materials based on CDs mainly improve the absorption of red light by plants, with limited absorption of blue light, so it is crucial to improve the quality of crops to concentrate the limited UV energy on increasing the absorption rate of blue light by plants.

Nitrogen atoms and carbon atoms have similar structures, similar radii, valence electrons with five chemical bonds, and a lone pair of electrons strongly attached to the sp2 carbon domain. Doping with nitrogen atoms can fundamentally affect the electronic structure of carbon atoms and further critically affect and improve the optical properties of CDs [25–27]. Therefore, it is a feasible strategy to effectively regulate the optical properties of CDs by doping an appropriate amount of nitrogen atoms and modulating the ratio of singlet excited state to triplet excited state (T1). With the modulation of ratio of singlet excited state to triplet excited state (T1), doping an appropriate amount of nitrogen atoms to effectively regulate the optical properties of CDs is a feasible strategy.

Based on the above ideas, N-doped carbon-dots (CNDs) (Fig. 1 ) were synthesized from triethylenetetramine hexaacetic acid (TTHA) via a one-step hydrothermal method. The CNDs can convert ultraviolet light to blue light (emission peak at 410 nm) that can be utilized in photosynthesis. Hence, the CNDs can enhance the light capturing ability of wheat seedling leaves, and significantly improve the overall photosynthesis efficiency and photosynthetic biomass of wheat seedlings.

Triethylenetetramine hexaacetic acid (TTHA, 0.8 g) and 30 mL of ultrapure water were put into a reaction kettle, which was fully stirred until dissolution. Then the kettle was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave, which was heated to 180 °C and maintained for 5 h. Then the autoclave was taken out and cooled to room temperature, forming a light yellow water solution. The solution was filtered by membranes (0.22 μm) and dialyzed by dialysis membranes (molecular weight cutoff was MWCO: 1000) for 48 h. Ultrapure water was changed every 4 h to remove the unreacted raw materials. Finally, the CNDs water solution in the dialysis membrane was collected, and freeze-dried to form white solid products.
See attachment support materials for experimental part.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) shows the average grain size of the CNDs is about 3.0 nm, without evident lattice fringes (Fig. 2 A), indicating these CNDs may be polymer carbon dots. X-ray diffraction (XRD) demonstrates the diffraction peak of the CNDs is 22 ° (Fig. S1A), suggesting that covalent a crosslinking skeleton was formed from polymerization inside the carbon dots [28] and further verifying these CNDs are polymer carbon dots.

The surface chemical structures of CNDs were observed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). XPS shows the CNDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, corresponding to three characteristic peaks at 285.1 eV (C1s), 399.1 eV (N1s) and 532.1 eV (O1s) respectively (Fig. 2B). Particularly, the C1s high-resolution energy spectrum (Fig. 2C) is split into three characteristic peaks at 284.4, 286.1 and 288.7 eV, which are ascribed to CC/CC, CN/CO and COOH respectively. The high-resolution O1s energy spectrum (Fig. 2D) is divided into 531.8 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to C-OH and CO. The high-resolution N1s energy spectrum (Fig. 2E) is divided into 399.4 and 401.8 eV, which are attributed to pyrrolic N and NH respectively. Besides, the surface functional groups of CNDs were further analyzed by FT-IR (Fig. S1B). As can be seen from the FT-IR images, the surface of the CNDs is rich in oxygen and nitrogen groups. The 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) shows CNDs have aliphatic (Sp3) carbon atoms (30–60 ppm) and carboxyl/amido groups (160–185 ppm) (Fig. 2F). The 1H NMR reveals the presence of Sp2-hybrid carbon in CNDs (Fig. S1C), indicating covalent crosslinking frame structure inside CNDs during the synthesis.
The above results indicate that a type of polymeric CNDs, enriched in oxygen and nitrogen groups at the surface, has been synthesized here.

The fluorescence properties of CNDs in aqueous solutions have been analyzed by studying their UV–vis spectra, photoluminescence spectra, and fluorescence lifetime decay curves. The UV spectra of CNDs in aqueous solutions show the peak at 330 nm due to n-π* transition of conjugated CO/CN, and the optimal emission peak is 410 nm (Fig. 3 A). CNDs under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp display blue fluorescence (inset at right upper corner in Fig. 3A). Since the excitation spectrum and absorption spectrum are overlapped at large extent (Fig. 3B), the absorption peak of CNDs at 330 nm corresponding to the chemical structure of CO/CN is also caused by the photoluminescence. This result further indicates that the absorption band edge of CNDs is 400 nm, and the corresponding forbidden bandwidth is 2.56 eV (Fig. 3C). Since CNDs have no obvious crystal structure, their fluorescence emission mainly originates from the surface and molecular state, and the mechanism of fluorescence emission may be the conjugation and structural distortion between the surface-enriched carboxyl groups and conjugated C atoms of CNDs that narrow down the energy level band gap of CNDs, inducing visible fluorescence emission. Moreover, because of the dependence on excitation, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 334 and 410 nm respectively (Fig. 3D). The average fluorescent lifetime (fitted from a double-exponential function) under the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths is 4.186 ns (Fig. 3E), and the fluorescent quantum yield is 0.36. The dependence on excitation and multi-exponential decay further suggests that CNDs as-synthesized may have multiple emission channels or emission centers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of CNDs promote photosynthesis of wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) seedlings were cultured in water. Then CNDs were spread onto the leaf surface of wheat seedlings to study the photosynthetic, physiological and ecological effects of CNDs on wheat seedlings (Fig. 4 A). The absorption spectrum of chloroplasts in the seedlings is concentrated at 380–500 nm (green lines), while the fluorescence emission spectrum of CNDs is focused within 380–550 nm (Fig. 4B and Fig. 3D), which is well overlapped with the absorption spectrum of chloroplasts. In comparison, the absorption spectrum of CNDs is concentrated within 280–400 nm (Fig. 3A). Hence, CNDs can convert the ultraviolet region (280–400 nm) of solar light rapidly into visible light that can be utilized by photosynthesis of wheat seedlings (Fig. 3D). Apparently, the CNDs can relieve damages of ultraviolet light to leaves of wheat seedlings, and provide sufficient light for the leaves, which significantly accelerate the photosynthetic rate of seedling leaves and the nitrogen use efficiency during the photosynthesis (both P < 0.05) (Fig. 4C). Besides, the growing indices of leaves are closely correlated to the times of CND spreading on leaves (CND concentration in the mother solution = 20 mg/ml). After 3 times of CND spreading, the root length, leaf area (Fig. 4D), dry leaf weight, total dry weight (Fig. 4E), root fresh weight, leaf fresh weight, and total fresh weight (Fig. 4F) are all significantly maximized (all P < 0.05). These results suggest that CNDs can significantly improve the biomass of wheat seedlings through the enhanced photosynthesis. However, the specific leaf area does not change significantly (Fig. 4G) (P > 0.05), maybe the leaf area and leaf dry weight increase simultaneously, leading to the insignificant change in specific leaf area. It can be seen from the above results that CNDs can promote the photosynthesis and growth of wheat seedlings.

To clarify the mechanisms about the changes in physiological and ecological indices of wheat seedlings, we detected 2, 6-dichlorophenolindophenol (DCPIP) reduction speed through Hill reaction and further studied the photosynthetic activity of chloroplast (Chl)/CNDs compounds. Since DCPIP is highly affinitive to the electron transferred from photosynthetic system II (PSII) to photosynthetic system I (PSI), DCPIP can be used to detect the activity of PSII [29]. By taking the simulated solar light (LED) as the light source, the DCPIP decreasing rate is gradually increased with the prolonging of illumination time, and slowly stabilized after 6 min. The DCPIP decreasing rate is maximized when the CND concentration is 6 mg/mL (Fig. 4H). According to these results, we can find that the Chl/CNDs compound under a certain concentration can significantly promote the activity of PSII.

When the photoreaction of photosynthesis occurred, the produced electrons were transferred by electron transport chains to PSII and PSI. In PSI, the excited electrons were transported to ferredoxin. After that, the electrons were transferred back to the electron transport chain of PSII, providing a circulation path for generation of ATP. Alternatively, the electrons can be utilized to reduce NADP+ to nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH). Thus, with the presence of an artificial electron acceptor, it can stop the electrons passing the electron transport chain, in order to determine the reduction rate and electron transfer rate. For this reason, iron cyanide reduction experiments were conducted to compare the electron transmission rates between Chl and Chl/CNDs compound (Fig. 4I). Results show the CNDs can largely accelerate the electron transfer and offer more chemical power for synthesis of ATP, forming more ATP. RubisCO was a key carboxylase that controls carbon assimilation during the photosynthesis, and its activity directly affected the photosynthetic productivity [30–31]. After processing with CNDs, the activity of RubisCO in wheat seedlings was improved by 3.44 times, and RubisCO enhanced the carbon assimilation ability of wheat seedlings (Fig. 4J), which may be related to the optical enhancement. Noticeably, after the CNDs/Cy3.5 on leaf surface were washed away with double-distilled water and the background fluorescence was modulated to black, abundant CNDs/Cy3.5 (red) were still fixed on leaf cells (Fig. 4Ka-c). Furthermore, the fluorescent signals of CNDs/Cy3.5 were mainly located in leaf veins and nearby mesophyll systems, the possible reason is that the CNDs first entered leaves via stomata and then entered veins via conducting tissues. SEM images (Fig. 4Kd-f) show the leaf cells cannot directly absorb CNDs. This is because wheat leaves contain abundant hydrophobic wax on the surface, so the CNDs were speculated to enter veins via stomata.

In summary, the mechanism by which CNDs nanomaterials promote photosynthesis in wheat seedlings may be explained as follows: chloroplasts can efficiently absorb the emission spectrum of CNDs and enhance the separation efficiency of optically excited electron and hole pairs during the photosynthesis. Consequently, PS II can capture more light to decompose water into more electrons that can be used in the electron transfer chain. After that, the electrons are transferred to adjacent protein compound cytochromes b6f and PS I, and then the protons drive the ATP synthase to produce ATP (Fig. 4L). Thus, CNDs can enhance the optical absorption, light capture, exciton quantity and photosynthetic electron transfer, thereby promoting the photosynthesis and increasing the photosynthetic productivity of wheat seedlings.

Compared with other carbon dots used for photosynthesis [20–21,30,32], the blue light quantum yield of CNDs in this study is higher, which can promote the synthesis of plant proteins and is more conducive to the improvement of crop quality (see Table 1 ).

To better evaluate the cytotoxicity of CNDs, we studied the interaction between CNDs and cells. The CNDs were first stained with amido Cy3.5 (Ex576 / Em604) and applied into Hela cells. The interaction between the CNDs and the cells was observed under laser confocal microscopy. Results showed the Hela cells incubated with free Cy3.5 only emitted weak red fluorescence (left in Fig. 5 A) from 1 to 16 h, but the cells cultured with Cy3.5/CNDs emitted significantly stronger red fluorescence (right in Fig. 5A), indicating the ingestion ability of Cy3.5/CNDs by Hela cells was strong.

The cytotoxicity at different concentrations of CNDs (0, 2, 200, 2000, 5000, 10000 μg/mL) were measured by a standard MTT method (Fig. 5B). Results demonstrated that at CNDs ≤ 200 μg/mL, the CNDs nearly did not affect the cell survival rate, indicating CNDs ≤ 200 μg/mL had high biocompatibility. Moreover, ROS and cell apoptosis were analyzed, which showed the CNDs almost did not affect the survival of Hela cells (Fig. 5C, 5D). The above results suggest CNDs at certain concentrations are non-cytotoxic to Hela cells.

CNDs with fluorescence quantum yield of 0.36 were prepared from a one-step hydrothermal method. These CNDs can improve the photosynthetic efficiency and photosynthetic biomass of wheat seedlings, and have low cytotoxicity. Therefore, it is crucial to construct artificial photosynthesis systems to increase the efficiency of solar energy capture and conversion by plants and reduce the threat of UV light.

Jinzhi Lv: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft. Wenli Yang: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration. Yanming Miao: Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was completed under the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant 32001202, 32171393 and 31700862).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122763.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
